item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Eh…

The logic here is a bit weird with regard to the obfuscation. It begs the question of "If the Foundation is hiding, who are they hiding from?" The IRS? Well, they're doing a real piss poor-job of it by taking $2,000,000 of an account that only had $40,000 in it.

Come to think of it, there's no case in which that magic money account presents a good front. It's an obvious illusion that collapses under even the faintest inspection. An account that never goes up or down in value, at a static number, and is subject to frequent withdrawals? If you're going to funnel your drug money into one account, at least change the money.

And Gerdinel's logic is weird too: who's he writing this report for? Clearly, somebody in the Foundation is aware of the account, if they're withdrawing money from it. Therefore, there's documentation for it elsewhere, he just doesn't have clearance for it … so he's giving himself away at digging into classified materials above his paygrade. A surefire way to get terminated, which is exactly what happens to him.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The investigations of sherbuck holmes…heh

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yea. This is just kind of…eh.

Guy sees that the bank fund amount never actually changes no matter how much they pull out, or put into, it. Guy then instantly assumes it's an scp rather than just the Foundation hiding its assets in a manner that is easy for them to access. I mean really. If they withdraw two million dollars from an account that has only 40k in it…doesn't that hint pretty heavily that the face amount is a lie and they know it?

Also, how did he "convince" the clerk to give out heavily sensitive payment information? You can't just hand wave that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So the foundation is laundering drugs, and a naive researcher has discovered evidence and decided to… Classify this as an anomaly with access restricted to people who would be in on it?

So now, rather than having to lie about celebrity donors and megacorporations, if anyone gets too nosy, they can say "Actually it's an anomaly, 3203, we classed it Thaumiel." Why do the people in charge not tell the researcher "Wow, congratulations, we hadn't really figured that all out, good job. Yeah, this is pretty important, thank you. In fact, it's so important that we have to keep it really secret, so here's some amnestics, and a reassignment to whatever project you'd like that would keep you way too busy."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My problem isn't with the concept, nor with the idea of an anomalous source of funding (I pretty much headcanon that the Foundation has lots of anomalous resources, including financial, personnel, and otherwise). My problem is that it breaks the conceit in a fundamental way.

Simply put, there's no reason this should be on a list in-universe. It was proposed and rejected. There should be no files, no details, the number itself should be unassigned or assigned to an approved anomaly. It being here is purely and entirely for the readers looking into the mythos, and does not logically fit into the mythos itself. It throws me right out of the illusion of the Foundationverse as its own self-contained entity, and there's not really anything that can be realistically done about that.

Although you received suggestions to the contrary, I think this should have been a tale rather than a mainlist SCP. As a tale, it's not subject to these limitations, and would not be irreconcilable with in-universe operation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I thought the concept was cool, although it did have some problems.And I love really weird items with a hint to a darker meaning at the end.Then I thought it was great.
In addition,the fake companies of the foundation were commonly abbreviated as SCP.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Pretty much what everyone else said. The use of Thaumiel breaks my suspension of disbelief and the object itself is just a generic magic item that doesn't answer the "where does the money come from?" question in an engaging way. -1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This was made to answer a valid issue often asked by many who engage in the SCP as writers and fans. I also like the premises of a proposed SCP, which is usually not seen outside the 001 slot.

Ultimately, the execution falters. Given that Thaumiel is regarded as a top secret object class, it is outright odd if a junior researcher knows it well enough to apply it in a proposal. The plot itself follows a clear-cut "deny and regroup" scheme that offers nothing new to readers.

Certainly, more can be done with an anomalous bank account to make it truly anomalous and distinctive.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like the concept, but this make me think that the nature of the foundation would mean they could get anything they want on the grounds of "We're a secret organization and if we don't get a thing, the world will be destroyed".

No vote for now

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: ^ What he said.

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
After recieving confirmation from Magnus, I've applied standard tags to this article.

The community has more decisive power of what should make the mainlist than staff, and you can't argue with votes. Regardless of whether Captain Kirby reposts this as a tale or not, it will remain on the mainlist.

item.title: Re: Interim Tech Captain Post
item.content:encoded:
Even then, they're 001 proposals and are expected to be boundary-breaking,

No they aren't, they're meant to be interesting stories. They just so happen to break the boundaries.

At any rate, I don't understand why this is even being discussed. It has special containment procedures, it has an object class, it falls in line with the clinical tone that is being set forth by the modern standards of this site — so, what's the big deal? And as Shaggy detailed above, we have articles that don't even fall within the realm of being in the SCP database.

Plus, if we're going to say that this article isn't an SCP but a tale, why would it be allowed to take up the space for an SCP article? We have a history of deleting articles that fall out of range, so why wouldn't we start deleting articles that aren't supposed to be there? I'm pretty sure if someone posted a GOI formatted article into the database, it would be deleted for not belonging there.

item.title: Mod Post
item.content:encoded: I have serious concerns as a moderator that this course of action is overstepping the mandate that technical team has in assigning tags. If this is a course you seriously think worth pursuing, I would ask that it be discussed on 05 as it is incredibly contrary to how things have operated in the past.

item.title: Re: Interim Tech Captain Post
item.content:encoded:
They key component every example you've cited has is thheir feasibility in being an actual entry in the Foundation database, that the Mainlist is supposed to represent.

Alright. Sure. How about SCPs which are very much not as they appear in the database, such as SCP-2254, which is captured documentation being commented on by the Serpents Hand?

Hell, what about SCP-3602, which isn't even an SCP file, but an Extranormal Event?

And who cares about the in-universe status by he end??? That'd be like saying Mr Wrong's proposal shouldn't be an 001 because by the end it's being moved into a normal mainlist slot.

Regardless of my feelings on this piece, I'm confused as to why this is even a discussion. It was posted in a mainlist slot, which it is retaining, and is titled appropriately. It was voted on by a couple dozen people as an SCP. The community overall has accepted this as an SCP entry, as evidenced by its positive rating.

Honestly, this is just nitpicking due to structure. If it lead off with the SCP proposal, would this even have been contested? I'd tag it by the SCP entry's parameters (thaumiel, monetary, etc) and call it a day.

item.title: Re: Interim Tech Captain Post
item.content:encoded:
They key component every example you've cited has is thheir feasibility in being an actual entry in the Foundation database, that the Mainlist is supposed to represent. 765 and 1949 are standard format with unorthodox concepts. 579 and 257-ARC feature heavy expungment that isn't unusual for documentation (and much more common for the period they were written in): 257-ARC clearly is a skull, and 579 most definitely follows format while being abstract as SCP-2719. 2763 and 2864 fall under "anomalies affecting their documentation", which has been accepted many times before.

However, this article isn't doing any of the above. It's a selection of internal communication between employees, and not something one would expect to show up on the "database" at all. There's a PROPOSAL for an article, but the sequential messages imply that the proposal doesn't even hold weight as an actual document, let alone anomaly.

It would have been better to cite shaggy or Bright's 001 proposals as an example, which feature literal story-telling on what's supposed to be a mockup of one of the Foundation's most coveted secrets. Even then, they're 001 proposals and are expected to be boundary-breaking,

The votes do speak for themselves here, though it does show how far we're reaching just for original takes on execution. I'm thinking this needs to be discussed by staff before officially labeling this as a tale— I haven't even seen input from Captain Kirby yet.

item.title: Re: Interim Tech Captain Post
item.content:encoded:
I have issues with this statement. What's worse, I have qualms.

How does this merit special scrutiny as opposed to any of the other 122 SCPs marked with the "Meta" tag? First point solved:

Almost all tags require an actual object to determine its effects.

Give it the "meta" tag, because this is a meta skip.

This is the part I have a problem with especially:

There's no object with special containment procedures, or even an anomaly.

SCP-765 is heavily implied to be non-anomalous. SCP-1949 has no spelled-out anomaly (and I spent so long ranting about that SCP that I had to excuse myself from the site for a few weeks), neither does:

  • SCP-257-ARC, aka the origin of the phrase "Does the Black Moon howl?" Technically gone from the site, but the fact that it's an archived skip implies it has some value.
  • SCP-579, whose description just reads [DATA EXPUNGED], and we're told it's anomalous, but now how it's anomalous.
  • SCP-2864, which literally has a bunch of researchers notes about whether or not it's anomalous and no proper skip designation?

Not to mention: this is sitting at +22. Not a great rating, but not likely to get downvoted to oblivion any time in the next week, at least. I don't see the technical team posting on whether or not SCP-2763 qualifies as a skip or not, and that has even less of a skip-like format than this does.

Why does this deserve to be designated as "not a skip", exactly? It has containment procedures, and having an explicit anomaly (or an anomaly at all) is optional, and this can, at the very least, be given the "meta" tag, and if not the "thaumiel" tag, then at least "unclassed". So, I ask again: why does this deserve special scrutiny?

item.title: Re: Interim Tech Captain Post
item.content:encoded:
No. It fits the list designation.

It's something, in-universe, which was filed as an SCP. Just 'cause it was 'confirmed' to be not an anomaly doesn't mean it wasn't given a designation.

If anything, it should be an -EX. Personally, I think it should stay here.

Also, that's outside my purview technically. That'd be up to the mods/admins not me.

item.title: Re: Interim Tech Captain Post
item.content:encoded: Quick question, if it's technically a "tale" shouldn't it be removed from the SCP mainlist?

item.title: Interim Tech Captain Post
item.content:encoded:
I was asked in a PM to come here, and provide a final call on if this deserves a tag of 'SCP' or not.

Agreeing with Accelerando's assessment here, this doesn't merit the SCP tag. There's no object with special containment procedures, or even an anomaly.

I'm not sure what this could actually be tagged with, frankly. Almost all tags require an actual object to determine its effects.

For now, I'm tagging this as tale, as that's the only tag which actually fits here. If there's an actual anomaly, feel free to PM me again.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm fine with your example being on the mainlist (and I'm a fan of that article) because there is definitely an anomaly, a mask, that's reason for the format screw.

Here, there's an exchange of emails proposing the existence of an article to be put in the database. I'm not opposed to something like this existing on the site, but it definitely needs more to it than what's here and implied.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ show block
– hide block

This isn't really an SCP article at all, but a tale with a proposal for one (that doesn't even exist).

Though I'm certainly not a fan, I think there's enough precedence to consider this as a mainlist entry. *cough cough* SCP-2864

This is a unique and interesting way of going about an SCP article

Unique? Not really. I could probably find half a dozen that incorporate interdepartmental memos into the format. This isn't exactly that wild, or much of a stretch.

Interesting? That's debatable. It came off as contrived (in the sense that this information was so readily attainable) and quite frankly, I was bored to death by the lack of anything resembling a compelling narrative.

More deets:

+ show block
– hide block

It's answering a question that, sure, plenty have asked over the years, but not in a way that

  • A) gives me the slightest reason to care about any of the characters (whole plot is kicked off on a lark, and set into motion with just as much naïveté and isn't really resolved in any manner which suggests there was anything lost or gained)
  • B) gives me anything to chew on regarding the wider scope or implications of the story (or lack thereof). Even if it's about the SCPF peddling drugs, hell, I could buy that, but that angle doesn't even seem to be present in the article, so I'm left with generic vagueness and nothing to consider.

If someone were to ask me "hey, remember that one SCP where they might maybe be hinting at selling drugs except nothing happens and everyone goes home?" I'd answer 'no'.

If you think this is to be lauded as unique and interesting then you're really missing out. Sorry about that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: No vote, for most of the same problems as above (kinda boring explaination etc, you can tell it was a concept to post in under 24hrs etc) but it was still pretty entertaining anyway.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nobody ever said you had to do one or the other.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I've often had this thought - where the fuck is all the money coming from? - but this really doesn't feel like a satisfactory answer. I tend to headcanon that the Foundation doesn't use anomalies to generate resources it needs, because anomalies are unpredictable by nature, and things produced by them could easily be cursed in a way that would go unnoticed until it's too late (that's the in-universe reason; narratively, I think it's way more interesting for them not to have cosmic cheat codes that let them handwave all practical concerns). So I really wanted to upvote this for the concept, but can't get behind the execution.

"magic bank account" is obviously not that interesting, but the "they're just drug dealers" explanation behind it fell flat for me too. Drugs are big business, but the resources required by the Foundation would add up to a lot of the world's economy - even if they had a total monopoly, it's not a grandiose enough source of revenue in the end.

Also, establishing and protecting a monopoly over the global drug trade would, itself, be a monumental task that wouldn't leave much time for securing and containing anomalies.

I really dig the grittiness of this concept, but it does seem a lot more suited for a tale - this seems like kind of an alternate-universe interpretation of the Foundation as simpler and more corrupt. I could get into that, but you'd have to do a lot more buildup around it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I highly disagree. If anything other than strictly an SCP, this is just a format screw. This is a different way of telling the narrative that the author chose, and based on the voting from the community, it seems to work.

This is a unique and interesting way of going about an SCP article, and personally, I welcome it as a scip.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
When I was shopping this piece around for feedback and critique, I asked a few people if they thought this was more of an SCP or a tale. Everyone I asked agreed that this should be an SCP. Although, in retrospect it might have been a more entertaining read to follow Gerdinel's investigation closer, and write it as a tale.

Either way, I understand your view point, but I was told that this was indeed an SCP instead of a tale, which is why I posted it to the main list.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This isn't really an SCP article at all, but a tale with a proposal for one (that doesn't even exist). I'm extremely hesitant to tag it as the former.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This sort of short quick-answer response to "How does the Foundation gets all of its money?" fits more as a tale, and even then I'd think it's too engaging to upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Completely unnecessary format screw that appears to have been done for its own sake, in the service of one of the most boring possible explanations for how the Foundation gets its money ("magic anomaly of infinite resources"), executed in an unnecessarily confusing manner.

this one went from concept to post in just over 24hrs

It shows.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I have the same problem(s) with this article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I believe that there are a few misunderstandings here that make the Foundation come off as more inept than they are depicted.

Not to mention the fraudulent account can be found by just asking Ed from accounting (for one site) - is he in on it, and if not, how does he explain the figures? None of this makes sense.

Ed does not know of the Swiss Bank Account. There are 2 degrees of separation between that account and what is considered common knowledge in accounting (the official Foundation account is fed by fronting companies which are fed by the Swiss Bank Account). So, most of the people at the Foundation have no need to explain the numbers.

A Site Director admits that the Foundation is willing to kill its own personnel at the request of a celebrity donor - and no-one expects that to be a problem?

While I realize that "terminate" is generally used to mean kill when used by the Foundation, this was intended to be read as either "we will kill you" or "we will fire you". It was left intentionally vague, but in such a place that one could assume the latter.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: When I read it I didn't think drugs I thought The Factory. It would make a lot more sense then I think.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If it can't be drugs, than it isn't drugs. It could literally be anything.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm not really sure I get this. The Foundation comes across as comically inept.

  • The account is either actually anomalous, or the account records are being falsified. I'm going to assume the latter, because if it's actually anomalous then (a) why is it being used for drug money laundering; and (b) the whole drug angle becomes unnecessary.
  • But if that's the case, why is the Foundation falsifying internal documents that are not made public - there doesn't seem to be a need to do so. If the reason is to confuse internal investigators (such as Gerdinel), then it's a terrible attempt at fraud, as the entries are so obviously bogus that they arouse suspicion. Not to mention the fraudulent account can be found by just asking Ed from accounting (for one site) - is he in on it, and if not, how does he explain the figures? None of this makes sense.
  • A Site Director admits that the Foundation is willing to kill its own personnel at the request of a celebrity donor - and no-one expects that to be a problem? Isn't that a far greater issue than just drug-running?
  • Also, the Site Director's blank refusal is almost designed to create more suspicion, rather than defuse it. This is indeed amateur hour stuff.
  • Moving the drug production to Argentina distances it from Foundation resources, but brings it "closer to our target market". The target market can only be the US (no point trying to make money by selling drugs where drugs are already cheap), so that suggests that the Foundation has no/few resources in America. So the Foundation is mainly based in Europe/Asia in this universe? And how does moving production to Argentina protect against discovery? Presumably no-one is about to stumble across a amphetamine production facility with Foundation logos emblazoned on it? And the account was discovered through electronic searches, which can be done no matter where the drugs (or the people doing the searching) are located. So why does it matter where the drugs are produced?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My headcanon is that the Foundation uses anomalies to generate a lot of their resources so that the amount of money that they need is a lot lower than you would expect. They have a few SCP that they use to obtain D-class personnel since the world's supply of death row inmates is not enough. So they must have some other SCPs that they use to generate other resources. There are a few SCPs that generate things like gold, water, and food, for example. There are probably a lot of other creative ways to save money using SCPs that I haven't thought of.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My thoughts as well. Foundation as world's largest drug dealer.

I can't fit it into my headcannon though. What with having access to SCPs that can make material out of thin air, hands in government pockets, AIs that could probably blow through the stock market in fifteen minutes and all sorts of other Gen+ technology, the Foundation simply has too many potential sources of income to need to rely on drugs as more than one of dozens of side operations.

Plus, having 80% of your income traced to one never changing bank account is some serious amateur hour stuff.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ potential spoiler
drugs

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't understand what is going on in the last collapsible. It implying something that I don't get?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks. I missed that when I copied from the sandbox.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Fixed a formatting error (missing "}")

+1 though. Fantastic read

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Loved it in draft, love it on the mainlist. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Short and sweet, just like how i like em. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks! This is my first stab at a format screw. It's a simple one, but a screw nonetheless.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Unconventional, but pretty interesting. +1

item.title: SCP-3203: The Swiss Bank Account
item.content:encoded:
Hey guys. So… this one went from concept to post in just over 24hrs. But I got a lot of eyes on it. Speaking of which, I'd like to thank Mendelssohn, annoyingdog, serkov, toastedraptor, taylor_itkin, Gummy_dragon, not_a_seagull, mayoculpa, and ziul123 for taking a look at this.

I hope you enjoyed!
